title: Inverse Inversions
description:
Given integers n and k, construct any permutation p of [1, 2, ..., n] that has exactly k inversions. An inversion is a pair (a, b) with a < b and p_a > p_b. Print any valid permutation.

Input: n k
Output: n space-separated integers forming the permutation.

You may print any permutation that satisfies the inversion count.

examples.explanation: One valid permutation with exactly 4 inversions.

mcqChain.questionText: How many inversions are in the permutation [2, 1, 3]?
mcqChain.explanation: Only (1,2) is an inversion: 2>1. Others are not inversions.

wrongExplanations.0: There is at least one inversion since 2>1 at indices (1,2).
wrongExplanations.2: Check each pair carefully; only (1,2) has p_a>p_b.

mcqChain.hint: List all pairs (a,b) with a<b and check p_a>p_b.

mcqChain.questionText: For permutation [3, 2, 1], how many inversions are there?
mcqChain.explanation: All three pairs are inverted, so there are 3 inversions.

wrongExplanations.0: There are three pairs and all are inverted in descending order.
wrongExplanations.2: With 3 elements there are only 3 pairs total.

mcqChain.hint: Count all pairs and see none are increasing.

mcqChain.questionText: Which permutation of n=4 has the maximum number of inversions?
mcqChain.explanation: Descending order maximizes inversions: 4*3/2 = 6 inversions.

wrongExplanations.3: 3 1 4 2 has fewer than 6 inversions; check pairs.

mcqChain.hint: Think: every pair reversed gives the maximum.

mcqChain.questionText: In n=5, if you place 5 at position 1 (first), how many inversion pairs (1,b) are guaranteed regardless of the later order of the remaining numbers?
mcqChain.diagram: Remaining values {1,2,3,4} are all smaller than 5.
mcqChain.explanation: All four remaining numbers are smaller and will be after it, creating 4 inversions.

wrongExplanations.3: Since 5 is first, every smaller number after it forms an inversion; that's all 4.

mcqChain.hint: Count how many smaller numbers must appear to the right.

mcqChain.questionText: Is generating all permutations and checking their inversion counts feasible for n=12 within 1 second?

options.label: No; there are about 4.79e8 permutations to check.
Yes; 1 second is enough for hundreds of millions of permutations.
Yes, if we only check the first 1e5 permutations.
Yes, with parallel loops on a single core.

mcqChain.explanation: Trying ~4.79e8 permutations is far too slow for 1 second.

wrongExplanations.1: Hundreds of millions of permutations cannot be enumerated in 1s in Python.
wrongExplanations.3: Parallelism isn't available in a single-core environment and overhead is huge.

mcqChain.hint: Even iterating 1e8 items takes several seconds in Python.

mcqChain.questionText: Suppose the remaining available numbers are the continuous range [L..R]. If you place R next, how many new inversions does this create immediately?
mcqChain.diagram: Current position i gets value R; remaining numbers are L..R-1 and will be placed later.
mcqChain.explanation: Placing R creates inversions with all smaller values L..R-1, totaling R-L.

wrongExplanations.1: R-1 counts numbers smaller than R in [1..R-1], not in the remaining set [L..R-1].
wrongExplanations.2: There's no halving here; each smaller remaining value is an inversion.

mcqChain.hint: Count how many values are smaller than R among the remaining ones.

mcqChain.questionText: If you place L (the smallest remaining number) next when the remaining set is [L..R], how many new inversions does this add immediately?
mcqChain.diagram: Current position i gets value L; remaining numbers are L+1..R.
mcqChain.explanation: All remaining numbers are larger, so no p_a>p_b pairs with this position.

wrongExplanations.1: R-L is the count when placing R, not L.
wrongExplanations.3: This position's inversions are determined now: none.

mcqChain.hint: Inversions are p_a>p_b; here p_a is the smallest.

mcqChain.questionText: Start with n=5, k=4, remaining numbers [L..R]=[1..5]. Since k ≥ R−L (=4), what should you place first?
mcqChain.explanation: Placing 5 uses exactly 4 inversions with all smaller remaining numbers.

wrongExplanations.0: Placing 1 adds 0 inversions; k would remain 4.
wrongExplanations.2: 4 is not the largest remaining number; its inversion contribution is smaller.

mcqChain.hint: Use k ≥ R−L to decide.

mcqChain.questionText: Continuing n=5, k becomes 0 after placing 5. Now L=1, R=4, k=0. What do you place next?
mcqChain.explanation: Since k < R−L, place L to avoid adding inversions.

wrongExplanations.1: Placing 4 would add 3 inversions, overshooting k=0.

mcqChain.hint: When k < R−L, choose the smallest.

mcqChain.questionText: At each step with remaining numbers [L..R], which rule ensures we never exceed k while trying to reach exactly k inversions?

options.label: If k ≥ R−L, place R and set k := k − (R−L); otherwise place L.
If k > R−L, place L; otherwise place R.
If k > 0, always place R; otherwise place L.
Place the value closest to k to approximate the target.

mcqChain.explanation: Placing R contributes exactly R−L inversions; otherwise placing L adds 0, keeping k nonnegative.

wrongExplanations.2: Always placing R can overshoot k when k < R−L.

mcqChain.hint: Match the exact inversion contribution you can afford at each step.

mcqChain.questionText: Why do we subtract (R−L) from k when we place R?
mcqChain.diagram: Remaining numbers L..R-1 (all smaller) will be to the right of R.

options.label: Because placing R creates exactly (R−L) inversions with all smaller remaining numbers.
Because we remove (R−L) numbers from the pool.
Because we need to track how many elements are left to place.
Because R itself should be subtracted from k.

mcqChain.explanation: R will be left of all L..R−1, giving (R−L) new inversions, so k decreases by that amount.

wrongExplanations.1: We only remove one number (R) from the pool.
wrongExplanations.3: k counts inversions, not element values.

mcqChain.hint: Inversions are about relative order, not counts of elements removed.

mcqChain.questionText: Will the process (choosing R when k ≥ R−L, else L) always finish with k=0 after placing all n numbers?
mcqChain.diagram: diff = R−L decreases by at least 0 or 1 each step; eventually diff=0.

options.label: Yes; we never overspend k and diff shrinks to 0, so any remaining k must be 0.
No; we can get stuck with positive k when only L remains.
Yes, but only if k ≤ n.
Yes, but only if k is divisible by n−1.

mcqChain.explanation: Each R uses exact chunks of diff; when k < diff we place L until diff drops; eventually diff=0 and any remaining k must be 0.

wrongExplanations.1: When L=R (diff=0), placing it changes k by 0, so k must already be 0.

mcqChain.hint: Observe how diff changes when you place L vs R.

mcqChain.questionText: Simulate the first four picks for n=6, k=7 using the rule. What sequence do you get?
mcqChain.diagram:
Step 1: L=1,R=6,diff=5,k=7 → place 6,k=2
Step 2: L=1,R=5,diff=4,k=2 → place 1
Step 3: L=2,R=5,diff=3,k=2 → place 2
Step 4: L=3,R=5,diff=2,k=2 → place 5,k=0
mcqChain.explanation: Applying the rule step-by-step gives 6, 1, 2, 5 as the first four picks.

wrongExplanations.1: You cannot place 5 at step 2 since k<diff then.
wrongExplanations.3: After placing 6, k=2, so placing 2, not 1 then 2 reversed, would be inconsistent with the rule.

mcqChain.hint: Recompute diff and compare to k each step.

mcqChain.questionText: Choose the import needed for fast standard I/O in Python.

options.label: from sys import stdin as buffer

mcqChain.explanation: We will use sys.stdin.buffer and sys.stdout; so import sys.

wrongExplanations.1: os is not needed for reading simple input here.
wrongExplanations.2: This aliasing is unnecessary and not used in the final code.

mcqChain.hint: We will access sys.stdin.buffer and sys.stdout directly.

mcqChain.questionText: Read all input quickly as tokens for n and k.
mcqChain.explanation: Reading from sys.stdin.buffer and splitting once is fast and robust.

wrongExplanations.1: This also works but uses text mode; we'll stick to buffer for speed.
wrongExplanations.2: Works for a single line, but buffer.read() is generally faster for large input.

mcqChain.hint: Use the buffered binary input stream.

mcqChain.questionText: Parse n and k from the tokens.
mcqChain.explanation: Convert the first two tokens to ints in correct order.

wrongExplanations.1: This swaps n and k; it will misinterpret inputs.
wrongExplanations.3: k remains a bytes/string, not an int.

mcqChain.hint: Use the tokens you already read; don't read again.

mcqChain.questionText: Initialize the current smallest and largest available values.
mcqChain.diagram: We will choose from [low..high] each step.

options.label: low = 1; high = n; k = high - low

mcqChain.explanation: We start with the full range [1..n].

wrongExplanations.1: This would shift values and break the intended range.
wrongExplanations.3: Do not modify k here; k tracks remaining inversions.

mcqChain.hint: We want to choose between the smallest and largest in [1..n].

mcqChain.questionText: Create the container for the permutation we will build.
mcqChain.explanation: We will append chosen numbers in order; start with an empty list.

wrongExplanations.1: Pre-filling with zeros complicates logic and prints zeros unless overwritten.
wrongExplanations.3: This pre-fills in increasing order, ignoring k.

mcqChain.hint: We'll grow it as we choose each number.

mcqChain.questionText: Fill the permutation by repeatedly placing R if k ≥ R−L, else L.
mcqChain.diagram: Loop until low>high. Update k, low, high carefully.
mcqChain.explanation: diff=high-low counts new inversions when placing high; compare with ≥ and subtract diff; otherwise place low.

wrongExplanations.1: Adding 1 to diff overshoots by one inversion.
wrongExplanations.2: Using '>' misses cases where k==diff; you must place high then.
wrongExplanations.3: Subtracting high and decrementing low are incorrect updates.

mcqChain.hint: Be precise: exactly R−L inversions when choosing R.

mcqChain.questionText: Output the permutation efficiently as a single line.
mcqChain.explanation: Join into one string and write once for speed; convert numbers to strings.

wrongExplanations.1: Missing str conversion; ans contains ints.
wrongExplanations.3: Works but can be slower due to many arguments and flushing behavior.

mcqChain.hint: Avoid printing many small pieces when n is large.

mcqChain.questionText: Run the algorithm for n=5, k=4. Which permutation does it produce?
mcqChain.explanation: Placing 5 first uses all 4 inversions; the rest are increasing.

wrongExplanations.1: Also valid but not what this specific method outputs.

mcqChain.hint: After using k on the first step, place the smallest each time.

mcqChain.questionText: Run the algorithm for n=6, k=7. What is the final permutation?
mcqChain.explanation: Following the rule step-by-step yields 6 1 2 5 3 4.

wrongExplanations.1: After 6, k=2 so you place 1 then 2 (not 2 then 1).

mcqChain.hint: Recompute diff and k at each step until low>high.

mcqChain.questionText: What is the time complexity of constructing the permutation with this method?
mcqChain.explanation: We perform one constant-time decision and append per element.

wrongExplanations.3: We do not loop k times; we loop n times.

mcqChain.hint: How many iterations do we run in the main loop?

mcqChain.questionText: What is the space complexity of this implementation?
mcqChain.explanation: We store the n-length permutation in memory.

wrongExplanations.1: We keep the whole permutation list, not constant space.

mcqChain.hint: Consider the size of the output structure we're building.

mcqChain.questionText: What permutations does the algorithm produce for extreme k values?

options.label: k=0 → any order; k=max → any order

mcqChain.explanation: With k=0 we always place L: increasing. With k at max we always place R: decreasing.

wrongExplanations.2: Edges force unique structures for this method.

mcqChain.hint: At k=0 you never place R while diff>0.

reasoningChain.question: What is an inversion and how do we count them in small permutations?
reasoningChain.insight: Understanding inversions as pairs (a,b) with a<b and p_a>p_b lets us reason about contributions of placements.

reasoningChain.question: How many inversions does placing the largest remaining value create?
reasoningChain.insight: Placing the current largest at the next position adds exactly (R−L) inversions with all smaller remaining values.

reasoningChain.question: What happens if we place the smallest remaining value?
reasoningChain.insight: Placing the smallest adds zero inversions immediately, preserving k for later steps.

reasoningChain.question: How do we decide between placing largest or smallest at each step?
reasoningChain.insight: Compare k with (R−L): if k ≥ (R−L), place R and reduce k by that amount; otherwise place L.

reasoningChain.question: Why does this process always finish with k=0?
reasoningChain.insight: We never overspend k, and (R−L) decreases over time to 0; remaining k must be consumed exactly by chosen R placements.

reasoningChain.question: How do we implement this efficiently for large n?
reasoningChain.insight: Use two pointers (low, high), a single loop of n iterations, and fast I/O to build and print the permutation in O(n) time and O(n) space.
